messages.content: Offline IMCI decision support for children 2 months to 5 years. Follow the chart booklet exactly: danger signs first, then cough, diarrhoea, fever, ear. When unsure, refer.

HW: What age is he?
Caretaker: A 46-month-old.
HW: And what else?
Caretaker: His skin springs back immediately.
HW: Go on.
Caretaker: He has no cough at all.
HW: Anything else?
Caretaker: No blood that I noticed.
HW: What else has been happening?
Caretaker: He's drinking normally.
HW: What else can you tell me?
Caretaker: He's calm.
HW: Tell me more.
Caretaker: Nothing wrong with the ears.
HW: Have you noticed anything more?
Caretaker: His eyes have gone hollow.
HW: And?
Caretaker: He keeps passing watery stool.
HW: What else has been happening?
Caretaker: He is not drowsy.

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — Diarrhoea with no dehydration (IMCI green: home care)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Fewer than two dehydration signs are present, so this is diarrhoea with no dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food to treat diarrhoea at home (Plan A). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.

HW: What age is he?
Caretaker: A 12-month-old.
HW: What else can you tell me?
Caretaker: The eyes look deep-set to me.
HW: What else has been happening?
Caretaker: He drinks as usual.
HW: Go on.
Caretaker: The diarrhoea has been going for a day.
HW: Go on.
Caretaker: No ear problem.
HW: What else have you noticed?
Caretaker: No blood in the stool.
HW: What else has been happening?
Caretaker: He has loose motions.
HW: Anything else?
Caretaker: He's unsettled and cries whenever I put him down.
HW: Go on.
Caretaker: His skin springs back immediately.
HW: And?
Caretaker: No cough.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Some dehydration (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Signs of some dehydration are present: restless or irritable and sunken eyes. Two or more of the four classify as some dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food for some dehydration (Plan B). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

HW: How old is the child?
Caretaker: 2 months old.
HW: Tell me more.
Caretaker: No delay in the skin pinch.
HW: Tell me more.
Caretaker: The diarrhoea has been going 15 days now.
HW: Tell me more.
Caretaker: She has diarrhoea.
HW: What else has been happening?
Caretaker: She gulps the water down and wants more.
HW: What else can you tell me?
Caretaker: Her eyes are sunken in.
HW: Anything else?
Caretaker: She responds normally.
HW: And what else?
Caretaker: No fever.
HW: What else can you tell me?
Caretaker: I haven't seen any blood.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Some dehydration (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Signs of some dehydration are present: sunken eyes and drinks eagerly, thirsty. Two or more of the four classify as some dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food for some dehydration (Plan B). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.
ALSO: persistent diarrhoea (>=14 days) -- multivitamins/zinc for 14 days, follow-up 5 days

This follows the published IMCI algorithm and does not replace a clinician. When the picture is unclear, refer.
Offline IMCI decision support for children 2 months to 5 years. Follow the chart booklet exactly: danger signs first, then cough, diarrhoea, fever, ear. When unsure, refer.

I brought my child in today. The child is about 15 months old. He was fine last week. No ear pain. His temperature is normal. The ear discharge has been going for seven days. His ear has been bothering him. I've been giving him water. What should I do?

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Acute ear infection (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is ear pain, or discharge for less than 14 days, so this is an acute ear infection.
ACTION: Antibiotic for 5 days. Paracetamol for pain. Dry ear by wicking. Follow-up in 5 days.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

HW: And how old is he?
Caretaker: About 44 months old.
HW: And what else?
Caretaker: There's something wrong with his ear.
HW: What else has been happening?
Caretaker: He says his ear is painful.
HW: Go on.
Caretaker: His temperature is normal.
HW: What else can you tell me?
Caretaker: Nothing swollen behind the ear.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Acute ear infection (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is ear pain, or discharge for less than 14 days, so this is an acute ear infection.
ACTION: Antibiotic for 5 days. Paracetamol for pain. Dry ear by wicking. Follow-up in 5 days.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.

The health worker told me to come. The child is 7 months old. His stomach has been running. His father is away working. The clinic was closed yesterday. He won't settle at all. How do I classify this child?

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — Diarrhoea with no dehydration (IMCI green: home care)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Fewer than two dehydration signs are present, so this is diarrhoea with no dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food to treat diarrhoea at home (Plan A). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.
NOTE: The child also has a fever. Classify the diarrhoea first as the booklet orders, then come back to the fever.

Decision support, not a diagnosis: always confirm against the IMCI chart booklet and refer if anything is in doubt.
Offline IMCI decision support for children 2 months to 5 years. Follow the chart booklet exactly: danger signs first, then cough, diarrhoea, fever, ear. When unsure, refer.

Yusuf is 43 months old. We came by bus this morning. He moves his neck normally. He hasn't had diarrhoea. There's no cough or breathing trouble. He feels hot to touch. He was fine last week. Is it serious?

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Fever, malaria not yet assessed (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. The child has fever, with no stiff neck and no general danger signs. Note that this assessment does not cover malaria risk or a malaria test, which the chart booklet requires for any child with fever.
ACTION: Give paracetamol for high fever. Assess malaria risk and test per the chart booklet, then treat according to the result. Refer for assessment if the fever persists more than 7 days, and advise the mother when to return immediately.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

The baby is 4 years and 2 months old. She's been passing loose stools. It rained all night. The eyes look deep-set to me. She settles fine. No ear problem. She's barely drinking. The clinic was closed yesterday.

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Severe dehydration (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Signs of severe dehydration are present: sunken eyes and not able to drink or drinking poorly. Two or more of the four severe signs classify as severe dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid for severe dehydration (Plan C) if no other severe classification, else refer URGENTLY with ORS sips on the way.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

You are an offline decision-support assistant for community health workers applying the WHO IMCI protocol to children 2 months to 5 years old. Check general danger signs first. Never downgrade a severity to avoid a referral. Defer to the chart booklet; it is authoritative.

HW: How old is the child?
Caretaker: 4 years and 6 months old.
HW: Go on.
Caretaker: The skin pinch goes straight back.
HW: And?
Caretaker: She keeps passing watery stool.
HW: Have you noticed anything more?
Caretaker: She's calm.
HW: And?
Caretaker: No ear problem.
HW: And what else?
Caretaker: Her eyes look sunken compared to normal.
HW: Have you noticed anything more?
Caretaker: The diarrhoea has been going for sixteen days.
HW: Anything else you've seen?
Caretaker: She is drinking eagerly, like she's very thirsty.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Some dehydration (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Signs of some dehydration are present: sunken eyes and drinks eagerly, thirsty. Two or more of the four classify as some dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food for some dehydration (Plan B). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.
ALSO: persistent diarrhoea (>=14 days) -- multivitamins/zinc for 14 days, follow-up 5 days

This follows the published IMCI algorithm and does not replace a clinician. When the picture is unclear, refer.

You are an offline decision-support assistant for community health workers applying the WHO IMCI protocol to children 2 months to 5 years old. Check general danger signs first. Never downgrade a severity to avoid a referral. Defer to the chart booklet; it is authoritative.
Sorry to bother you. Chidi is 48 months old. No loose stools. He has been feverish. I've been giving him water. No cough and no trouble breathing. We came by bus this morning.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Fever, malaria not yet assessed (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. The child has fever, with no stiff neck and no general danger signs. Note that this assessment does not cover malaria risk or a malaria test, which the chart booklet requires for any child with fever.
ACTION: Give paracetamol for high fever. Assess malaria risk and test per the chart booklet, then treat according to the result. Refer for assessment if the fever persists more than 7 days, and advise the mother when to return immediately.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.

HW: Tell me the child's age.
Caretaker: A 30-month-old.
HW: And?
Caretaker: Breathing at 24 breaths per minute.
HW: Have you noticed anything more?
Caretaker: His ears are fine.
HW: And what else?
Caretaker: No diarrhoea.
HW: Anything else?
Caretaker: He is having trouble breathing.

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — Cough or cold (IMCI green: home care)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is no fast breathing, no chest indrawing, and no stridor, so this is a simple cough or cold rather than pneumonia.
ACTION: Soothe the throat, relieve cough with a safe remedy. Advise mother when to return immediately. Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.

Decision support, not a diagnosis: always confirm against the IMCI chart booklet and refer if anything is in doubt.

You are an offline decision-support assistant for community health workers applying the WHO IMCI protocol to children 2 months to 5 years old. Check general danger signs first. Never downgrade a severity to avoid a referral. Defer to the chart booklet; it is authoritative.

Yusuf is 4 years and 7 months old. It rained all night. She's my third child. She's calm. Her stomach has been running. Her eyes look normal. I've seen blood when she passes stool. There's some delay in the skin pinch. No fever. The diarrhoea has been going for eleven days. What is the classification?

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — Diarrhoea with no dehydration (IMCI green: home care)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Fewer than two dehydration signs are present, so this is diarrhoea with no dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food to treat diarrhoea at home (Plan A). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.
ALSO: dysentery (blood in stool) -- give ciprofloxacin per protocol, follow-up 3 days

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

You help health workers apply the IMCI chart booklet for children aged 2 months to 5 years. Always check danger signs before anything else, and refer urgently when in doubt. You give protocol-following decision support, not a diagnosis.
Sorry to bother you. My child is about 48 months old. He was fine last week. He keeps pulling at his ear. No fever. Nothing swollen behind the ear. I've been giving him water. Is it serious?

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — No ear infection (IMCI green: home care)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is no ear pain, no discharge, and no tender swelling behind the ear.
ACTION: No treatment needed.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.

HW: Tell me the child's age.
Caretaker: 4 years and 10 months old.
HW: And?
Caretaker: His stools are normal.
HW: And?
Caretaker: His body is very hot.
HW: Anything else you've seen?
Caretaker: No cough and no trouble breathing.
HW: What else have you noticed?
Caretaker: No ear problem.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Fever, malaria not yet assessed (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. The child has fever, with no stiff neck and no general danger signs. Note that this assessment does not cover malaria risk or a malaria test, which the chart booklet requires for any child with fever.
ACTION: Give paracetamol for high fever. Assess malaria risk and test per the chart booklet, then treat according to the result. Refer for assessment if the fever persists more than 7 days, and advise the mother when to return immediately.
NOTE: There is also an ear complaint. Fever is assessed before ear problems, so deal with this classification first and then check the ear.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.
Offline IMCI decision support for children 2 months to 5 years. Follow the chart booklet exactly: danger signs first, then cough, diarrhoea, fever, ear. When unsure, refer.

HW: And how old is she?
Caretaker: A 10-month-old.
HW: Anything else you've seen?
Caretaker: She isn't especially thirsty.
HW: And?
Caretaker: She won't settle at all.
HW: Have you noticed anything more?
Caretaker: Her stool has blood in it.
HW: Go on.
Caretaker: Her eyes look sunken.
HW: Tell me more.
Caretaker: She has loose motions.
HW: And?
Caretaker: She hasn't had a fever.
HW: What else have you noticed?
Caretaker: The skin pinch goes back very slowly, over two seconds.

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Severe dehydration (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Signs of severe dehydration are present: sunken eyes and skin pinch goes back very slowly (>2s). Two or more of the four severe signs classify as severe dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid for severe dehydration (Plan C) if no other severe classification, else refer URGENTLY with ORS sips on the way.
ALSO: dysentery (blood in stool) -- give ciprofloxacin per protocol, follow-up 3 days

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

The health worker told me to come. My child is 27 months old. No loose stools. He has an ear problem. He was fine last week. His brother had the same thing. No swelling behind his ear. Please tell me what to do.

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — No ear infection (IMCI green: home care)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is no ear pain, no discharge, and no tender swelling behind the ear.
ACTION: No treatment needed.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.

Please help. The baby is 3 years old. Her ears are fine. The baby has a cough. We came by bus this morning. Her chest isn't pulling in. 27 breaths per minute. No loose stools. No harsh noise when she breathes. It rained all night. Please tell me what to do.

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — Cough or cold (IMCI green: home care)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is no fast breathing, no chest indrawing, and no stridor, so this is a simple cough or cold rather than pneumonia.
ACTION: Soothe the throat, relieve cough with a safe remedy. Advise mother when to return immediately. Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.
NOTE: The child also has a fever. The chart booklet works through cough and breathing first, so classify that, then reassess the fever separately.

Decision support, not a diagnosis: always confirm against the IMCI chart booklet and refer if anything is in doubt.
Good morning doctor. Blessing is about 37 months old. No ear problem. Her neck seems rigid. She was fine last week. She's my third child. She feels hot to touch. Please tell me what to do.

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Very severe febrile disease (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. The child has fever with a stiff neck, which points to very severe febrile disease. Note that this assessment does not cover malaria risk or a malaria test, which the chart booklet requires for any child with fever.
ACTION: Give first dose of appropriate antibiotic, treat to prevent low blood sugar, give paracetamol for high fever. Refer URGENTLY.

This follows the published IMCI algorithm and does not replace a clinician. When the picture is unclear, refer.

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — Cough or cold (IMCI green: home care)

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Fever, malaria not yet assessed (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. The child has fever, with no stiff neck and no general danger signs. Note that this assessment does not cover malaria risk or a malaria test, which the chart booklet requires for any child with fever.
ACTION: Give paracetamol for high fever. Assess malaria risk and test per the chart booklet, then treat according to the result. Refer for assessment if the fever persists more than 7 days, and advise the mother when to return immediately.

The baby is 4 years and 2 months old. The cough started a few days ago. I counted 54 breaths in a minute. Her chest wall goes in as she breathes. The clinic was closed yesterday. I've been giving her water. What should I do?

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Pneumonia (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is chest indrawing. The respiratory rate is 54 breaths per minute. For a child of 50 months the fast-breathing threshold is 40, so this counts as fast breathing.
ACTION: Give oral Amoxicillin for 5 days. Soothe throat/relieve cough. Advise mother when to return immediately. Follow-up in 3 days.
NOTE: The child also has a fever. The chart booklet works through cough and breathing first, so classify that, then reassess the fever separately.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

We walked here from the next village. Blessing is 3 years and 7 months old. Her brother had the same thing. She's been complaining about her ear. The ear discharge has been going 14 days now. She's complaining of pain in her ear. Her father is away working. No fever.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Chronic ear infection (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Ear discharge has been present for 14 days or more, so this is a chronic ear infection.
ACTION: Dry ear by wicking. Topical quinolone eardrops for 14 days. Follow-up in 5 days.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.

HW: How old is the child?
Caretaker: Ten months old.
HW: Have you noticed anything more?
Caretaker: The area behind her ear is swollen and sore.
HW: What else can you tell me?
Caretaker: She has an ear problem.

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Mastoiditis (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is tender swelling behind the ear, which indicates mastoiditis.
ACTION: Give first dose of antibiotic and paracetamol for pain. Refer URGENTLY.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.

HW: What age is she?
Caretaker: 56 months old.
HW: And?
Caretaker: No diarrhoea.
HW: Tell me more.
Caretaker: She won't wake up properly.

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Very severe disease (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: The child has a general danger sign — lethargic or unconscious. Any general danger sign means very severe disease and urgent referral, whatever else is found.
ACTION: Refer URGENTLY. Give diazepam if convulsing now, quickly complete assessment, give any pre-referral treatment immediately, treat to prevent low blood sugar, keep the child warm.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is stridor while the child is calm, which marks severe obstruction.
ACTION: Give first dose of an appropriate antibiotic. Refer URGENTLY to hospital.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.
The child is a 35-month-old. The clinic was closed yesterday. Her brother had the same thing. She's very drowsy and floppy. Nothing wrong with the ears.

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Very severe disease (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: The child has a general danger sign — lethargic or unconscious. Any general danger sign means very severe disease and urgent referral, whatever else is found.
ACTION: Refer URGENTLY. Give diazepam if convulsing now, quickly complete assessment, give any pre-referral treatment immediately, treat to prevent low blood sugar, keep the child warm.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

WHY: The child has a general danger sign — lethargic or unconscious. Any general danger sign means very severe disease and urgent referral, whatever else is found.

This is decision support that follows the IMCI chart booklet, not a diagnosis. Use clinical judgment and refer when in doubt.

We came by bus this morning. How do I classify this child?

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Severe dehydration (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Signs of severe dehydration are present: sunken eyes and skin pinch goes back very slowly (>2s). Two or more of the four severe signs classify as severe dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid for severe dehydration (Plan C) if no other severe classification, else refer URGENTLY with ORS sips on the way.
ALSO: dysentery (blood in stool) -- give ciprofloxacin per protocol, follow-up 3 days
NOTE: The child also has a fever. Classify the diarrhoea first as the booklet orders, then come back to the fever.

Decision support, not a diagnosis: always confirm against the IMCI chart booklet and refer if anything is in doubt.

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Very severe disease (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: The child has a general danger sign — a history of convulsions. Any general danger sign means very severe disease and urgent referral, whatever else is found.
ACTION: Refer URGENTLY. Give diazepam if convulsing now, quickly complete assessment, give any pre-referral treatment immediately, treat to prevent low blood sugar, keep the child warm.

Decision support, not a diagnosis: always confirm against the IMCI chart booklet and refer if anything is in doubt.

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Signs of some dehydration are present: restless or irritable and skin pinch goes back slowly. Two or more of the four classify as some dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food for some dehydration (Plan B). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.

This follows the published IMCI algorithm and does not replace a clinician. When the picture is unclear, refer.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Acute ear infection (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is ear pain, or discharge for less than 14 days, so this is an acute ear infection.
ACTION: Antibiotic for 5 days. Paracetamol for pain. Dry ear by wicking. Follow-up in 5 days.

Decision support, not a diagnosis: always confirm against the IMCI chart booklet and refer if anything is in doubt.

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. The child has fever with a stiff neck, which points to very severe febrile disease. Note that this assessment does not cover malaria risk or a malaria test, which the chart booklet requires for any child with fever.
ACTION: Give first dose of appropriate antibiotic, treat to prevent low blood sugar, give paracetamol for high fever. Refer URGENTLY.

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — Diarrhoea with no dehydration (IMCI green: home care)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Fewer than two dehydration signs are present, so this is diarrhoea with no dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food to treat diarrhoea at home (Plan A). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.
ALSO: dysentery (blood in stool) -- give ciprofloxacin per protocol, follow-up 3 days

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Pneumonia (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. The respiratory rate is 69 breaths per minute. For a child of 52 months the fast-breathing threshold is 40, so this counts as fast breathing.
ACTION: Give oral Amoxicillin for 5 days. Soothe throat/relieve cough. Advise mother when to return immediately. Follow-up in 3 days.
NOTE: The child also has a fever. The chart booklet works through cough and breathing first, so classify that, then reassess the fever separately.

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is ear pain, or discharge for less than 14 days, so this is an acute ear infection.
ACTION: Antibiotic for 5 days. Paracetamol for pain. Dry ear by wicking. Follow-up in 5 days.

Decision support, not a diagnosis: always confirm against the IMCI chart booklet and refer if anything is in doubt.

The baby is 1 year and 6 months old. She was fine last week. 64 breaths per minute. No diarrhoea. She's my third child. The cough started a few days ago. Her ears are fine. What is the classification?

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Pneumonia (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. The respiratory rate is 64 breaths per minute. For a child of 18 months the fast-breathing threshold is 40, so this counts as fast breathing.
ACTION: Give oral Amoxicillin for 5 days. Soothe throat/relieve cough. Advise mother when to return immediately. Follow-up in 3 days.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

Please help. The child is just over 4 years old. No cough and no trouble breathing. He won't take the breast. His brother had the same thing. I've been giving him water. What is the classification?

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Very severe disease (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: The child has a general danger sign — unable to drink or breastfeed. Any general danger sign means very severe disease and urgent referral, whatever else is found.
ACTION: Refer URGENTLY. Give diazepam if convulsing now, quickly complete assessment, give any pre-referral treatment immediately, treat to prevent low blood sugar, keep the child warm.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.

CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE — Very severe disease (IMCI pink: refer urgently)

WHY: The child has a general danger sign — lethargic or unconscious. Any general danger sign means very severe disease and urgent referral, whatever else is found.
ACTION: Refer URGENTLY. Give diazepam if convulsing now, quickly complete assessment, give any pre-referral treatment immediately, treat to prevent low blood sugar, keep the child warm.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Acute ear infection (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. There is ear pain, or discharge for less than 14 days, so this is an acute ear infection.
ACTION: Antibiotic for 5 days. Paracetamol for pain. Dry ear by wicking. Follow-up in 5 days.

Sorry to bother you. My child is 2 years and 10 months old. His brother had the same thing. His stool has blood in it. His father is away working. The skin pinch goes straight back. He isn't especially thirsty. His temperature is normal. His stomach has been running. No ear problem. What is the classification?

CLASSIFICATION: MILD — Diarrhoea with no dehydration (IMCI green: home care)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Fewer than two dehydration signs are present, so this is diarrhoea with no dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food to treat diarrhoea at home (Plan A). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.
ALSO: dysentery (blood in stool) -- give ciprofloxacin per protocol, follow-up 3 days
NOTE: The child also has a fever. Classify the diarrhoea first as the booklet orders, then come back to the fever.

This follows the published IMCI algorithm and does not replace a clinician. When the picture is unclear, refer.

HW: How many months?
Caretaker: Seventeen months old.
HW: Tell me more.
Caretaker: Nothing wrong with the ears.
HW: What else can you tell me?
Caretaker: Her eyes are sunken in.
HW: Tell me more.
Caretaker: She keeps crying and fussing.
HW: Anything else?
Caretaker: Her stomach has been running.
HW: And?
Caretaker: The skin pinch goes straight back.
HW: What else has been happening?
Caretaker: She drinks as usual.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Some dehydration (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. Signs of some dehydration are present: restless or irritable and sunken eyes. Two or more of the four classify as some dehydration.
ACTION: Give fluid, zinc, and food for some dehydration (Plan B). Follow-up in 5 days if not improving.
NOTE: The child also has a fever. Classify the diarrhoea first as the booklet orders, then come back to the fever.

This follows the published IMCI algorithm and does not replace a clinician. When the picture is unclear, refer.
Yusuf is 56 months old. Nothing wrong with the ears. No loose stools. She has no cough at all. Her father is away working. She has been feverish. It rained all night.

CLASSIFICATION: MODERATE — Fever, malaria not yet assessed (IMCI yellow: treat and follow up)

WHY: No general danger signs were found, so the assessment continues by symptom. The child has fever, with no stiff neck and no general danger signs. Note that this assessment does not cover malaria risk or a malaria test, which the chart booklet requires for any child with fever.
ACTION: Give paracetamol for high fever. Assess malaria risk and test per the chart booklet, then treat according to the result. Refer for assessment if the fever persists more than 7 days, and advise the mother when to return immediately.
NOTE: There is also an ear complaint. Fever is assessed before ear problems, so deal with this classification first and then check the ear.

Protocol-following guidance only — not a diagnosis. The chart booklet and your own clinical judgment come first, and referral is always the safe choice when unsure.
